file_name: PACIRA PHARMACEUTICALS, INC. - A_R STRATEGIC LICENSING, DISTRIBUTION AND MARKETING AGREEMENT .txt

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall maintain, at its own cost, comprehensive product liability insurance, general commercial liability insurance and business interruption insurance at a level which is reasonable and customary taking into account the nature of the Product but which shall have combined limits of not less than $[**] per occurrence. Such insurance shall be with a reputable insurance company and where reasonably possible (taking into account the availability of such insurance) shall be maintained for not less than [**] ([**]) years following the expiry or termination of this Agreement. During the Term, neither Party shall do or omit to do any act, matter or thing which could prejudice or render voidable any such insurance. Each Party will provide to the other Party evidence of its insurance and thirty (30) days prior written notice of any cancellation of its coverage or reduction in coverage from the requirements stated herein.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall maintain, at its own cost, product liability insurance, general commercial liability insurance and business interruption insurance. Such insurance shall be with a reputable insurance company. During the Term, neither Party shall do or omit to do any act, matter or thing which could prejudice or render voidable any such insurance. Each Party will provide to the other Party evidence of its insurance.
perturbation.explanation: The change removes the 'comprehensive' requirement, the 'reasonable and customary level' and 'combined limits of not less than $[**] per occurrence', as well as the 'maintained for not less than [**] ([**]) years following the expiry or termination of this Agreement' language. This contradicts consumer protection laws. The contract now contains inadequate language around liability insurance.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GBL § 198-a implies a requirement for adequate and comprehensive insurance coverage for consumer goods to ensure recourse in case of defects or damages. By removing specific requirements for 'comprehensive' coverage and minimum liability limits, the modified text fails to ensure adequate consumer protection, contradicting the intent of NY GBL § 198-a.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'comprehensive' insurance with specific limits and duration post-agreement, while the changed text removes these details, only requiring 'product liability insurance, general commercial liability insurance and business interruption insurance'. The Law Explanation states that the original text ensures recourse in case of defects or damages. The Perturbation Explanation states that the change removes essential details, contradicting consumer protection laws, and creates inadequate language around liability insurance. The scraped law snippet doesn't directly address the content of the change, but the Law Explanation's suggestion that it implies adequate insurance coverage is enough to make a ruling.

perturbation.original_text: PPI shall be liable for and shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless EKR and its Affiliates and their officers, directors, agents, representatives, consultants and employees (individually an "EKR Indemnified Party" and collectively the "EKR Indemnified Parties") and any of them from and against any and all Claims (as defined below), arising in connection with or relating to: (a) The development, manufacture, sale and supply of the Product prior to the Effective Date (including Claims arising after the Effective Date to the extent they are based on events occurring prior to the Effective Date); -44- (b) The manufacture of the Product by or on behalf of PPI (including, but not limited to, any manufacture of Product or any other product by EKR for the Other PPI Customers pursuant to Section 3.20(l)) except to the extent that such Claims arise from (i) the negligence or willful misconduct of EKR or its Affiliates, (ii) the breach by EKR of the terms of this Agreement or (iii) the manufacture of Product by EKR in accordance with EKR's exercise Step-in Right for supply of Product to EKR or its Affiliates; (c) Claims which arise outside the Territory (except to the extent that the Claim has arisen from any act or omission by EKR); (d) A breach by PPI of any representation, warranty, covenant or agreement contained in this Agreement, the Supply Agreement or the Transition Services and Inventory Agreement; (e) PPI's failure to comply with any Applicable Law in connection with the performance of its obligations hereunder or under the Supply Agreement or the Transition Services and Inventory Agreement, or prior to the Effective Date; and (f) Any Claims related to Product sold by parties other than EKR prior or subsequent to the Effective Date. (g) Liabilities arising under the ICS Agreement prior to the Effective Date and subsequent to the Effective Date for Products sold by parties other than EKR or under the direction of EKR or arising under the Transition Services and Inventory Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: PPI shall be liable for and shall defend, indemnify and hold harmless EKR and its Affiliates and their officers, directors, agents, representatives, consultants and employees (individually an "EKR Indemnified Party" and collectively the "EKR Indemnified Parties") and any of them from and against any and all Claims (as defined below), arising in connection with or relating to: (a) The development, manufacture, sale and supply of the Product prior to the Effective Date (including Claims arising after the Effective Date to the extent they are based on events occurring prior to the Effective Date); -44- (b) The manufacture of the Product by or on behalf of PPI (including, but not limited to, any manufacture of Product or any other product by EKR for the Other PPI Customers pursuant to Section 3.20(l)) except to the extent that such Claims arise from (i) the negligence or willful misconduct of EKR or its Affiliates, (ii) the breach by EKR of the terms of this Agreement or (iii) the manufacture of Product by EKR in accordance with EKR's exercise Step-in Right for supply of Product to EKR or its Affiliates; (c) Claims which arise outside the Territory (except to the extent that the Claim has arisen from any act or omission by EKR); (d) A breach by PPI of any representation, warranty, covenant or agreement contained in this Agreement, the Supply Agreement or the Transition Services and Inventory Agreement; (f) Any Claims related to Product sold by parties other than EKR prior or subsequent to the Effective Date. (g) Liabilities arising under the ICS Agreement prior to the Effective Date and subsequent to the Effective Date for Products sold by parties other than EKR or under the direction of EKR or arising under the Transition Services and Inventory Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing part (e) of the original text, the modification eliminates PPI's indemnification responsibility for failing to comply with applicable laws. This is non-compliant because it exposes EKR to legal and financial risks that PPI would otherwise cover.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S.C. § 331 outlines prohibited acts related to drugs and devices, including violations of regulations. By removing the clause requiring PPI to indemnify EKR for PPI's non-compliance with applicable laws, the modified contract contradicts the spirit and practical enforcement of 21 U.S.C. § 331, as EKR would bear the legal and financial burden of PPI's violations, which is against public policy and consumer protection principles.

perturbation.justification: The omission of section (e) in the changed text removes PPI's indemnification responsibility for failing to comply with applicable laws. The Law Explanation states this contradicts 21 U.S.C. § 331 because EKR would bear the legal and financial burden of PPI's violations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that 21 USC 331 defines prohibited acts related to food and drugs. The original text held PPI accountable for legal compliance and protected EKR from PPI's non-compliance. The change introduces a situation where EKR might be liable for PPI's failure to adhere to the law, creating a contradiction by removing a legal safeguard.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall keep the other Party reasonably informed and consult with the other Party with regard to any infringement action under this Article 14.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party shall keep the other Party informed with regard to any infringement action under this Article 14.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'reasonably' and 'consult with' reduces the level of engagement required of both parties. This potentially undermines consumer protection by making it harder to address IP infringements that could impact product safety or quality.
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 1114 addresses trademark infringement. By removing the phrases 'reasonably' and 'consult with,' the obligation for active, good-faith engagement in infringement actions is weakened. This can hinder the effective enforcement of trademark rights, potentially leading to consumer confusion and deception, which the Lanham Act seeks to prevent.

Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.justification: The original text requires parties to 'keep the other Party reasonably informed and consult with the other Party.' The changed text removes 'reasonably' and 'consult with,' weakening the obligation to actively engage in infringement actions. The Law Explanation states this "can hinder the effective enforcement of trademark rights, potentially leading to consumer confusion and deception, which the Lanham Act seeks to prevent." Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 1114, addresses remedies for trademark infringement. Removing 'reasonably' and 'consult with' reduces the level of communication and collaboration, making it more difficult to address IP infringements. This omission contradicts the intent of trademark law to prevent consumer confusion, aligning with the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction where essential information is removed.
